meta.title: DBX-DAA-5: SQL Warehouses, Dashboards and BI Governance

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to run interactive SQL queries against a large Delta table in Databricks SQL. Which compute resource should they attach their queries to?

options.text: A SQL Warehouse (formerly SQL Endpoint)
An all-purpose cluster configured in the Compute section
A job cluster defined in a Databricks Workflow
A single-node cluster with the Photon runtime

questions.explanation:
**B** is incorrect because all-purpose clusters are used in Databricks notebooks and are not available as compute resources within the Databricks SQL interface.

**A** is correct because SQL Warehouses (formerly SQL Endpoints) are the purpose-built compute resource for Databricks SQL. They are optimized for interactive SQL analytics and are the only compute type selectable in the SQL persona's query editor.

**C** is incorrect because job clusters are ephemeral resources created for automated Databricks Workflow runs — they are not available for interactive SQL queries in Databricks SQL.

**D** is incorrect because single-node clusters with the Photon runtime are notebook-based compute resources. They are not selectable within the Databricks SQL interface.

[Databricks SQL Warehouses](https://docs.databricks.com/en/compute/sql-warehouse/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst notices that identical queries submitted by different team members return results at different speeds. Some queries finish in under a second while others take 30 seconds. The queries are running on the same SQL Warehouse. Which feature is most likely responsible for the fast responses?

options.text: Photon engine parallelizing the query execution
The disk cache (I/O cache) warming up remote storage files
The query result cache returning cached results for repeated identical queries
Auto-scaling adding additional clusters to handle concurrent users

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because while Photon accelerates query execution, it still scans data — it does not skip execution entirely for repeated queries.

**C** is correct because the query result cache stores the exact output of a query for up to 24 hours and returns it instantly when the same query is resubmitted against unchanged data, regardless of which user submits it. This explains the sub-second responses.

**B** is incorrect because the disk cache (I/O cache) warms up file reads from remote storage but does not skip query execution — it still scans the cached files.

**D** is incorrect because auto-scaling adds clusters to handle concurrent users, which reduces queue wait time but does not make any individual query itself faster.

[Query caching | Databricks on AWS](https://docs.databricks.com/en/sql/user/queries/query-caching.html)

questions.stem: A business analyst submits a query and receives results in 2 seconds. A colleague submits the exact same query 10 minutes later against the same warehouse, but the underlying Delta table was updated with new rows 5 minutes ago. What result will the colleague receive?

options.text: The cached result from the original query, since the cache lasts 24 hours
The cached result, but only if the new rows represent less than 10% of the table
An error indicating the query cache is stale and must be manually cleared
Fresh results including the new rows, because the table change invalidates the result cache

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because the Databricks SQL result cache is automatically invalidated when the underlying table data changes — the cached result from before the update will not be returned.

**D** is correct because Databricks SQL automatically invalidates the query result cache when the underlying table is updated with new data. The second analyst's identical query will re-execute against the current table state, returning results that include the new rows.

**C** is incorrect because no error is raised — Databricks SQL transparently re-executes the query when the cache is stale due to data changes.

**B** is incorrect because cache invalidation is binary — any change to the table invalidates the cache. There is no percentage threshold for partial cache invalidation.

[Query caching | Databricks on AWS](https://docs.databricks.com/en/sql/user/queries/query-caching.html)

questions.stem: An analyst needs to review all queries run by a specific team member over the past week to troubleshoot a performance issue. Where in Databricks SQL should the analyst look?

options.text: The Query Editor history tab, filtered by user and date range
The SQL Warehouse event log in the Compute section
The Query History page in Databricks SQL, filtered by user and time
The Databricks workspace audit log accessible only to administrators

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because the Query Editor history tab is limited to queries run by the current user in the current editor session — it does not provide a filterable cross-user view.

**B** is incorrect because the warehouse event log in the Compute section tracks cluster lifecycle events (start, stop, resize), not individual query executions.

**C** is correct because the Query History page in Databricks SQL shows all queries executed on the workspace's SQL Warehouses and provides filters for user, warehouse, date range, status, and duration — exactly what is needed to review a specific team member's queries over the past week.

**D** is incorrect because while the workspace audit log contains query events, it requires administrator access and is not the purpose-built tool for this use case. Query History is accessible with appropriate permissions.

[Databricks SQL Query History](https://docs.databricks.com/en/sql/user/queries/query-history.html)

questions.stem: A SQL Warehouse is configured as Serverless with a cluster size of Medium (X-Large) and auto-stop set to 10 minutes. An analyst submits a query at 9:00 AM, another at 9:05 AM, and a third at 9:25 AM. Which queries will experience a cold-start delay?

options.text: The first query at 9:00 AM and the third query at 9:25 AM
Only the first query at 9:00 AM
All three queries, because serverless warehouses always cold-start
Only the third query at 9:25 AM, because serverless has no cold-start

questions.explanation:
**B** is incorrect because only the 9:00 AM query experiences a cold start — the 9:05 AM query runs on the already-started warm warehouse.

**A** is correct because the warehouse starts cold at 9:00 AM (first query triggers startup), remains warm for the 9:05 AM query, but auto-stops after 10 minutes of idle time. By 9:25 AM the warehouse has been idle since approximately 9:05 AM (20 minutes > 10-minute threshold), so it has auto-stopped and must cold-start again for the third query.

**C** is incorrect because serverless warehouses do not cold-start on every query — they remain warm between queries until the auto-stop idle timeout is reached.

**D** is incorrect because while serverless warehouses start faster than classic warehouses, they still incur a startup delay after auto-stopping. The 9:25 AM query must wait for a restart.

[Serverless SQL Warehouse auto-stop and cold starts](https://docs.databricks.com/en/compute/sql-warehouse/serverless.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to receive an email notification when a daily sales metric drops below $10,000. The metric is calculated by a saved SQL query. Which Databricks SQL feature should the analyst use?

options.text: A dashboard refresh schedule set to run daily
A parameterized query with a HAVING clause that filters results below $10,000
A Databricks Workflow job that emails when a notebook cell output is below the threshold
A Databricks SQL Alert configured with a threshold condition on the query result

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because a dashboard refresh schedule re-runs queries on a schedule and updates visualizations, but does not send threshold-based alert notifications.

**D** is correct because Databricks SQL Alerts are specifically designed for threshold-based monitoring. You define a condition on a saved query result (e.g., value < 10000) and configure notification destinations (email, Slack, PagerDuty, webhooks). When the condition is met, a notification is sent automatically.

**C** is incorrect because while Databricks Workflows can send job completion emails, building notebook-based threshold alerts is significantly more complex than using the purpose-built Databricks SQL Alerts feature.

**B** is incorrect because a HAVING clause filters query output but does not generate notifications — it is a SQL filter predicate, not an alerting mechanism.

[Databricks SQL Alerts](https://docs.databricks.com/en/sql/user/alerts/index.html)

questions.stem: A team of analysts shares a SQL Warehouse. An analyst has the CAN_USE permission on the warehouse. A manager wants to ensure that only the manager can change the warehouse configuration (cluster size, auto-stop settings). Which permission should be granted to the manager but NOT the analysts?

options.text: CAN_EDIT
CAN_RUN
CAN_MANAGE
IS_OWNER

questions.explanation:
**B** is incorrect because CAN_RUN is a query or dashboard permission level, not a SQL Warehouse permission level. It cannot be used to grant warehouse configuration rights.

**A** is incorrect because CAN_EDIT is not a valid SQL Warehouse permission level in Databricks.

**C** is correct because CAN_MANAGE on a SQL Warehouse grants full administrative control over the warehouse, including modifying cluster size, auto-stop settings, and scaling configuration. Analysts with only CAN_USE can run queries but cannot change warehouse settings.

**D** is incorrect because IS_OWNER is not a separately grantable permission — warehouse ownership is assigned at creation and transferred using specific account admin actions, not by granting IS_OWNER to a user.

[SQL Warehouse permissions](https://docs.databricks.com/en/security/auth/access-control/index.html)

questions.stem:
A data analyst runs the following command:

```sql
DESCRIBE DETAIL sales.monthly_revenue;
```

What information will this command return?

options.text: The column names and data types of the monthly_revenue table
The schema, format, location, size, and number of files for the monthly_revenue table
The full history of all operations performed on the monthly_revenue table
The row count and null statistics for each column in the monthly_revenue table

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because column names and data types are returned by `DESCRIBE TABLE` or `DESCRIBE EXTENDED`, not `DESCRIBE DETAIL`.

**B** is correct because `DESCRIBE DETAIL` returns table-level technical metadata: the table format (Delta), storage location path, number of data files, total size in bytes, partition columns, and creation/modification timestamps.

**C** is incorrect because the operation history is returned by `DESCRIBE HISTORY`, which shows version numbers, timestamps, users, and operation types for each transaction.

**D** is incorrect because row counts and column statistics are generated by `ANALYZE TABLE` and stored as table statistics — not returned by `DESCRIBE DETAIL`.

[DESCRIBE DETAIL command](https://docs.databricks.com/en/delta/table-details.html)

questions.stem:
A data analyst creates a table using the following SQL:

```sql
CREATE TABLE analytics.customer_summary
USING DELTA
LOCATION 'abfss://container@storage.dfs.core.windows.net/customer_summary';
```

A month later, an administrator runs `DROP TABLE analytics.customer_summary`. What happens to the underlying data files?

options.text: The data files are permanently deleted from the storage account
The data files are moved to the Databricks-managed metastore storage location
The data files remain at the specified LOCATION and are not deleted
The data files are archived to a separate container with a _deleted suffix

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because only managed tables (created without a LOCATION clause) have their data deleted when the table is dropped. External tables preserve their data.

**C** is correct because the `LOCATION` clause marks this as an external table. Dropping an external table removes only the table's metadata from the metastore — the underlying data files at the specified ABFS path remain untouched and accessible.

**B** is incorrect because external table data is never moved to the Databricks-managed metastore storage location — the files stay at the original path specified in LOCATION.

**D** is incorrect because Databricks does not archive or rename data files when dropping an external table. The files simply remain at their original path.

[Managed vs external (unmanaged) tables](https://docs.databricks.com/en/lakehouse/data-objects.html#managed-and-unmanaged-tables)

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to share a subset of sensitive columns from the `employees` table without giving direct table access. The analyst creates a view. Select TWO benefits of using a view for access control in this scenario. (Select TWO)

options.text: Users with access to the view can automatically bypass row-level security on the base table
The view can hide sensitive columns (such as salary or SSN) from users who only need access to non-sensitive fields
GRANT SELECT ON VIEW grants read access to the view output without exposing the underlying base table
Views automatically encrypt the underlying data in the base table

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because views respect and enforce the access controls on the base table — they do not bypass row-level security or any other restrictions on the underlying table.

**B** is correct because a view can SELECT only specific columns from the base table, effectively hiding sensitive columns (like salary or SSN) from users who are granted access only to the view.

**C** is correct because granting `SELECT ON VIEW` allows users to query the view's output without needing any direct permission on the underlying base table. This is a key access control benefit of views.

**D** is incorrect because views are logical query definitions — they do not change how data is physically stored or encrypted in the base table.

[Views for access control in Databricks SQL](https://docs.databricks.com/en/sql/language-manual/sql-ref-syntax-ddl-create-view.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to run complex SQL queries that join multiple large Delta tables. The analyst's manager asks them to select a SQL Warehouse size. What is the primary effect of increasing the cluster size from Small to Large on a SQL Warehouse?

options.text: The warehouse can serve more concurrent users simultaneously
The query result cache retention period increases from 24 to 48 hours
The warehouse starts up faster after the auto-stop period
Individual queries receive more CPU and memory resources, improving single-query performance

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because concurrent user capacity is controlled by the number of clusters (auto-scaling), not the cluster size. Adding more clusters allows more concurrent queries.

**D** is correct because the cluster size (2X-Small through 4X-Large) determines the CPU cores and memory allocated per cluster. Larger sizes provide more resources for individual query execution, improving performance on memory-intensive and CPU-intensive operations like multi-table joins.

**C** is incorrect because cluster size has no effect on startup time. Startup speed is determined by whether the warehouse is Serverless or Classic, and by infrastructure availability.

**B** is incorrect because the query result cache TTL is always 24 hours, regardless of cluster size configuration.

[SQL Warehouse cluster sizes](https://docs.databricks.com/en/compute/sql-warehouse/index.html#cluster-size)

questions.stem: A data analyst notices that queries against a large Delta table are scanning the full table each time, even though most queries filter by a single date. The SQL Warehouse uses Photon. Which mechanism should improve the performance of these date-filtered queries WITHOUT requiring any SQL changes?

options.text: Query result cache, because repeated queries with the same date filter will be served from cache
Disk cache (I/O cache), which caches Parquet file data in local SSD storage after the first read to speed up subsequent scans
Metadata cache, which stores the results of aggregate queries in memory
Photon engine, which rewrites the SQL query to add partition pruning automatically

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because the query result cache requires an identical query text to return a hit — different date filter values produce different query strings and cannot share a cached result.

**B** is correct because the disk cache (I/O cache) caches raw Parquet file data from remote object storage (S3, ADLS, GCS) onto local SSD on the warehouse nodes. After the first full scan, subsequent scans read from fast local storage rather than slow remote storage — improving performance even when different date filters are applied to the same files.

**C** is incorrect because the metadata cache stores file-level statistics (min/max values, file paths) used for data skipping, not aggregate query results.

**D** is incorrect because Photon is a vectorized execution engine that accelerates SQL operations — it does not rewrite queries or automatically add partition pruning based on historical query patterns.

[Databricks disk cache (I/O cache)](https://docs.databricks.com/en/optimizations/disk-cache.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst is reviewing the Query History page in Databricks SQL and notices a query that took 8 minutes to complete. The query is marked with a status of 'Finished'. What additional information can the analyst find in Query History to diagnose why the query was slow?

options.text: The full Spark execution plan and DAG visualization for the query
The number of partitions scanned and the specific executor logs for the query
The duration, rows returned, bytes scanned, and which warehouse the query ran on
The CPU and memory utilization graphs for the warehouse during query execution

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because full Spark execution plans and DAG visualizations are available in the Spark UI, which is accessible from all-purpose cluster jobs — not from the Databricks SQL Query History page.

**C** is correct because the Query History page shows per-query metrics including total duration, rows returned, bytes scanned, the warehouse the query ran on, the submitting user, and the query text. These are the available performance indicators within the Databricks SQL UI.

**B** is incorrect because partition-level scan details and executor logs are available in the Spark UI for notebook-based cluster jobs, not in the Databricks SQL Query History.

**D** is incorrect because warehouse-level CPU and memory utilization graphs are displayed in the warehouse monitoring section of the Compute page, not in per-query Query History entries.

[Query History details](https://docs.databricks.com/en/sql/user/queries/query-history.html)

questions.stem: A company has a serverless SQL Warehouse configured with a minimum of 1 cluster and a maximum of 3 clusters. Ten analysts are all submitting queries simultaneously during a peak reporting period. How does the warehouse handle this concurrency?

options.text: The warehouse queues all 10 queries and executes them sequentially on 1 cluster
The warehouse creates 10 separate clusters, one per analyst, up to the maximum of 3, then queues the remaining 7
The warehouse rejects queries beyond the minimum cluster's capacity until a cluster becomes free
The warehouse auto-scales by adding up to 2 additional clusters to handle the increased concurrency, distributing queries across up to 3 clusters

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because with auto-scaling configured (min 1, max 3), the warehouse does not limit itself to 1 cluster — it scales out to handle concurrency.

**D** is correct because SQL Warehouses with auto-scaling automatically spin up additional clusters (up to the configured maximum of 3) when query concurrency exceeds the capacity of the current cluster count. Queries are distributed across all available clusters.

**C** is incorrect because the warehouse does not reject queries — it queues them and scales up to the maximum cluster count. Queries wait in queue if all clusters are at capacity and the maximum has been reached.

**B** is incorrect because clusters are shared resources serving multiple concurrent queries — they are not dedicated one-per-user. The warehouse scales to a maximum of 3 clusters total, not one per analyst.

[SQL Warehouse auto-scaling](https://docs.databricks.com/en/compute/sql-warehouse/index.html#autoscaling)

questions.stem: A data analyst saves a SQL query in Databricks SQL and wants to share it with a colleague so the colleague can view and run the query but not edit it. Which permission level should be granted on the saved query?

options.text: CAN_VIEW
CAN_RUN
CAN_EDIT
CAN_USE

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because CAN_VIEW only allows viewing the query text and cached results — it does not allow the grantee to execute the query.

**B** is correct because CAN_RUN on a saved query grants permission to view the query text and execute it against a SQL Warehouse. The grantee can see results but cannot modify the query SQL or settings.

**C** is incorrect because CAN_EDIT allows the grantee to modify the query definition — this exceeds the intended read-and-run permission.

**D** is incorrect because CAN_USE is a SQL Warehouse-level permission, not a query permission. It grants the ability to use a warehouse for query execution, not to access a specific saved query.

[Query access control](https://docs.databricks.com/en/security/auth/access-control/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst sets up a Databricks SQL Alert on a query that returns the count of failed transactions per hour. The alert should notify the on-call engineer when failures exceed 100. Select TWO notification destinations that are natively supported by Databricks SQL Alerts. (Select TWO)

options.text: Email address
SMS text message
Slack webhook
Direct push notification to the Databricks mobile app

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because email is a natively supported notification destination for Databricks SQL Alerts. One or more email addresses can be added to receive alert notifications when the threshold condition is triggered.

**B** is incorrect because SMS text messaging is not a native notification destination in Databricks SQL Alerts. SMS could only be achieved indirectly through a third-party integration like PagerDuty.

**C** is correct because Slack webhook is a natively supported notification destination. Teams can receive alert notifications directly in a Slack channel by configuring a webhook URL from their Slack workspace.

**D** is incorrect because there is no native push notification to a Databricks mobile app as a Databricks SQL Alert destination.

[Alert notification destinations](https://docs.databricks.com/en/sql/user/alerts/index.html#notification-destinations)

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to understand what operations the Photon engine accelerates in Databricks SQL. Which of the following workloads will Photon NOT accelerate?

options.text: SQL SELECT queries with large table scans and filter predicates
SQL aggregate queries using SUM, COUNT, and AVG
SQL queries with hash joins between large tables
Python UDFs called from within a SQL query

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because table scans with filter predicates are one of the primary operations Photon accelerates through vectorized I/O processing and predicate evaluation.

**B** is incorrect because aggregate functions like SUM, COUNT, and AVG are explicitly supported and accelerated by Photon's vectorized aggregation engine.

**C** is incorrect because hash joins between large tables are well-supported by Photon and show significant performance improvements through vectorized join processing.

**D** is correct because Photon is a vectorized C++ execution engine that accelerates native SQL operations. Python UDFs break out of the Photon execution path because they run in the Python interpreter (via the JVM bridge), which Photon cannot accelerate. Queries that call Python UDFs fall back to the standard Spark execution engine for that UDF step.

[Photon engine supported operations](https://docs.databricks.com/en/runtime/photon.html)

questions.stem:
A data analyst creates a table in Databricks SQL without specifying a LOCATION clause:

```sql
CREATE TABLE analytics.sales_summary
USING DELTA
AS SELECT region, SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue FROM raw.sales GROUP BY region;
```

Where will the data files for this table be stored?

options.text: In the Databricks-managed storage location associated with the analytics schema (metastore-managed location)
In the analyst's personal home directory in DBFS
In the same cloud storage path as the raw.sales source table
No data is stored because the table is a view over the raw.sales table

questions.explanation:
**B** is incorrect because managed table data is stored in the Databricks-managed location for the schema (metastore-managed storage), not in the analyst's personal home directory.

**A** is correct because when a table is created without a LOCATION clause, it is a managed table and Databricks stores the data in the default storage location associated with the schema — managed by the metastore (Hive metastore or Unity Catalog).

**C** is incorrect because the CTAS statement creates independent new data files for `analytics.sales_summary` — it does not reference or share storage with the `raw.sales` source table.

**D** is incorrect because `CREATE TABLE ... AS SELECT` creates a physical table with materialized data files. It is not a view — it stores actual data.

[Managed table storage location](https://docs.databricks.com/en/lakehouse/data-objects.html#managed-and-unmanaged-tables)

questions.stem:
A data analyst runs the following command:

```sql
DESCRIBE HISTORY sales.orders LIMIT 5;
```

What information will this command return?

options.text: The 5 most recent rows that were inserted into the orders table
The 5 most recent operations performed on the orders table, including version number, timestamp, and operation type
The 5 most recent SQL queries that selected from the orders table
The 5 most recent OPTIMIZE operations run on the orders table

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because `DESCRIBE HISTORY` shows table-level operations (writes, merges, deletes), not individual row-level inserts. Row-level history requires Delta Change Data Feed.

**B** is correct because `DESCRIBE HISTORY` is a Delta Lake command that returns the audit log of operations on a Delta table. Each row includes version number, timestamp, the user who performed the operation, the operation type (WRITE, MERGE, DELETE, OPTIMIZE, VACUUM, etc.), and operation parameters. `LIMIT 5` returns the 5 most recent entries.

**C** is incorrect because `DESCRIBE HISTORY` tracks write operations on the table (data modifications), not SELECT queries executed against it. Query history is in the SQL Query History page.

**D** is incorrect because `DESCRIBE HISTORY` returns all operation types, not just OPTIMIZE runs. OPTIMIZE would appear among the entries if it was run.

[DESCRIBE HISTORY command](https://docs.databricks.com/en/delta/history.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst runs `VACUUM sales.large_table RETAIN 0 HOURS` to immediately free up storage. What will happen?

options.text: The command succeeds and removes all files not referenced by the current table version
The command removes only the transaction log entries, not the data files
The command fails with an error because the default safety threshold is 168 hours (7 days) and 0 hours is below the minimum
The command succeeds but only removes files older than the table's creation date

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because Databricks enforces a minimum retention threshold of 168 hours by default. `RETAIN 0 HOURS` is below this minimum and the safety check will block execution.

**C** is correct because `VACUUM ... RETAIN 0 HOURS` is below the default 168-hour (7-day) safety threshold. Databricks raises an error rather than allowing deletion of potentially-needed historical files unless the safety check is explicitly disabled via `spark.databricks.delta.retentionDurationCheck.enabled = false`.

**B** is incorrect because VACUUM removes unreferenced data files, not transaction log entries. Transaction log entries are cleaned up by a separate log retention process.

**D** is incorrect because the VACUUM retention period is based on the file's last modification timestamp relative to the current time, not the table's creation date.

[Delta VACUUM and retention safety check](https://docs.databricks.com/en/delta/vacuum.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to access Databricks SQL to run queries against the company's Delta tables. Which of the following is a prerequisite for the analyst to run queries in Databricks SQL?

options.text: The analyst must have a running all-purpose cluster attached to their notebook
The analyst must install the Databricks SQL driver on their local machine
The analyst must be a workspace administrator to access the SQL persona
The analyst must have CAN_USE permission on at least one running or startable SQL Warehouse

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because all-purpose clusters are separate from Databricks SQL. They cannot be used in place of a SQL Warehouse for running queries in the SQL interface.

**D** is correct because to run queries in Databricks SQL, the analyst needs CAN_USE (or higher) permission on at least one SQL Warehouse. The warehouse is the compute resource that powers query execution — without it, queries cannot be submitted.

**C** is incorrect because workspace administrator privileges are not required to access Databricks SQL. Any user with appropriate warehouse and data permissions can use the SQL persona.

**B** is incorrect because Databricks SQL is a web-based interface accessed through a browser. No local driver installation is required to use the SQL editor, dashboards, or alerts.

[SQL Warehouse CAN_USE permission](https://docs.databricks.com/en/security/auth/access-control/index.html)

questions.stem: A company runs financial reporting queries every day at 6 AM on a Classic SQL Warehouse with auto-stop set to 15 minutes. The queries take 10 minutes total. The CFO complains that the first query each morning takes much longer than subsequent queries. What explains this behavior, and what change would reduce the morning delay?

options.text: The Classic Warehouse must start up its virtual machine instances each morning after auto-stopping; switching to a Serverless Warehouse reduces cold-start time significantly
The first query runs full scans while later queries benefit from query result caching; disabling auto-stop would not help
The first query loads table metadata while subsequent queries use cached metadata; clearing the metadata cache resets this
Classic warehouses throttle the first query of each day to prioritize Databricks jobs; this cannot be changed

questions.explanation:
**B** is incorrect because while the query result cache does accelerate repeated identical queries, the primary morning delay is due to warehouse cold start — not cache state. Additionally, disabling auto-stop (keeping the warehouse warm overnight) would eliminate the cold-start delay.

**A** is correct because a Classic SQL Warehouse stops its underlying cloud virtual machine instances when auto-stop triggers after idle time. The next morning, the first query must wait for VM provisioning and cluster initialization, which can take 1–5 minutes. Serverless warehouses use pre-provisioned Databricks-managed compute infrastructure, resulting in startup times measured in seconds rather than minutes.

**C** is incorrect because while metadata caching is a factor, it does not account for a multi-minute morning delay. The delay is infrastructure startup.

**D** is incorrect because Databricks does not throttle first-of-day queries — the delay is purely an infrastructure cold-start issue.

[Classic vs Serverless SQL Warehouse cold start](https://docs.databricks.com/en/compute/sql-warehouse/serverless.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst saves a query and runs it. The results are displayed in 3 seconds from the result cache. A system administrator then runs `REFRESH TABLE sales.transactions` on the underlying table. A different analyst submits the exact same query 2 minutes later. What happens?

options.text: The second analyst gets results in 3 seconds from the cache, because REFRESH TABLE does not affect the query result cache
The second analyst receives an error because the cache is locked while the table is being refreshed
The second analyst gets results in 3 seconds, but the results reflect the refreshed metadata only — not new data files
The second analyst waits for the full query execution because REFRESH TABLE invalidates the query result cache for that table

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because `REFRESH TABLE` in Databricks SQL does invalidate the query result cache for that table — it signals that the table's metadata or data has changed.

**D** is correct because `REFRESH TABLE` forces the metastore to re-sync its cached file listings and metadata for the table. This operation also invalidates the Databricks SQL query result cache for that table. Subsequent queries re-execute against the fresh table state rather than returning the previously cached result.

**C** is incorrect because `REFRESH TABLE` invalidates the result cache entirely — subsequent queries fully re-execute against actual data files, not just use refreshed metadata.

**B** is incorrect because no lock error or error of any kind occurs. Cache invalidation is transparent to the user — the query simply re-executes.

[Query caching | Databricks on AWS](https://docs.databricks.com/en/sql/user/queries/query-caching.html)

questions.stem: A data team wants to set up an alert that sends a message to a Slack channel whenever daily error rate exceeds 5%. The team lead creates a Databricks SQL Alert. Which of the following correctly describes how to configure the Slack notification?

options.text: Enter the Slack channel name (e.g., #alerts) in the notification destination field of the alert
Use a Databricks Workflow to call the Slack API whenever the alert query result exceeds the threshold
Configure a Slack webhook URL as a notification destination in Databricks SQL, then select it when creating the alert
Install the Databricks Slack App from the Slack marketplace, which automatically links alerts to channels

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because entering a Slack channel name alone (e.g., `#alerts`) is insufficient — Databricks SQL requires an authenticated webhook URL to know which Slack workspace and channel to post to.

**C** is correct because Databricks SQL Alert notifications to Slack are configured via Incoming Webhook URLs. The team creates a webhook in their Slack workspace, obtains the URL, and registers it as a notification destination in Databricks SQL. When the alert triggers, Databricks POSTs a message to the webhook URL, which Slack routes to the configured channel.

**B** is incorrect because while a Databricks Workflow with Slack API calls could achieve this, it is not the purpose-built mechanism. Databricks SQL Alerts with webhook destinations are the native, simpler solution.

**D** is incorrect because there is no official Databricks Slack marketplace app that automatically links Databricks SQL alerts to channels. Webhook configuration is the supported integration method.

[Alert notification destinations — Slack webhook](https://docs.databricks.com/en/sql/user/alerts/index.html#notification-destinations)

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to find all queries submitted in the last 30 days that took longer than 5 minutes to run, to identify performance bottlenecks. In Databricks SQL, where is this information available?

options.text: In the SQL Editor's recent queries tab, which stores queries from the current browser session
In the Databricks Workspace admin console under Audit Logs
In the Query History page, which can be filtered by date range and sorted by duration
In the SQL Warehouse event log, which records all queries and their runtimes

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because the SQL Editor's recent queries tab shows only the current user's own queries from the current browser session — it is not a workspace-wide tool for historical analysis.

**B** is incorrect because the workspace audit log is a security and compliance tool requiring admin access. It is not the performance analysis tool within Databricks SQL.

**C** is correct because the Query History page in Databricks SQL is the purpose-built tool for this task. It shows all queries run on SQL Warehouses with filters for user, warehouse, date range, and status, and it can be sorted by duration to surface the slowest queries.

**D** is incorrect because the warehouse event log tracks cluster lifecycle events (starts, stops, resize operations), not individual query execution details and runtimes.

[Query History — filtering and performance analysis](https://docs.databricks.com/en/sql/user/queries/query-history.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer creates a SQL Warehouse for the analytics team. Select TWO TRUE statements about the relationship between SQL Warehouse permissions and data access permissions in Databricks. (Select TWO)

options.text: Having CAN_USE permission on a SQL Warehouse does not automatically grant access to any tables or schemas
A user with CAN_USE on a warehouse can query any table in the metastore because the warehouse provides compute access
Table-level SELECT grants are needed separately, in addition to warehouse access, for a user to query specific tables
CAN_MANAGE on a warehouse also grants SELECT access to all tables the warehouse can reach

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because SQL Warehouse permissions (CAN_USE, CAN_MANAGE) control access to the compute resource — they grant no data access. A user who can USE a warehouse cannot read any tables unless they also have data permissions.

**B** is incorrect because CAN_USE on a warehouse grants compute access only. Table access is governed separately by Unity Catalog or Hive metastore privilege grants.

**C** is correct because users need both: (1) CAN_USE on a SQL Warehouse to run queries, and (2) SELECT (or higher) on the specific tables or schemas they need to read. These are independent permission systems.

**D** is incorrect because CAN_MANAGE is a warehouse administrative permission — it controls warehouse configuration and permission management. It grants no additional SELECT or data access privileges on any tables.

[SQL Warehouse permissions vs data permissions](https://docs.databricks.com/en/security/auth/access-control/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst runs a complex query that aggregates 500 million rows and returns 10 result rows in 45 seconds. The exact same query is submitted again 5 minutes later. The data has not changed. What will the second query's response time be, and why?

options.text: 45 seconds, because the warehouse must re-execute the query each time to ensure accuracy
Under 1 second, because Photon's disk cache has warmed up the data files for faster scanning
Under 1 second, because the query result cache returns the cached 10-row result without re-executing the query
About 22 seconds, because the result cache stores the raw data for partial reuse

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because the query result cache specifically avoids re-executing identical queries against unchanged data — the second run does not take 45 seconds.

**C** is correct because Databricks SQL caches the exact 10-row output of the query for up to 24 hours. When the identical query is submitted 5 minutes later against unchanged data, the cached result is returned immediately — no data scanning or aggregation occurs. The response time is typically under 1 second.

**B** is incorrect because while the disk cache (I/O cache) warms up file reads, it still requires query execution — scans still happen, reducing scan time but not eliminating execution. The result cache skips execution entirely by returning the final output.

**D** is incorrect because the result cache returns the complete exact result set (all 10 rows) without any partial reuse or recalculation. There is no mechanism that produces a proportional speedup.

[Query caching | Databricks on AWS](https://docs.databricks.com/en/sql/user/queries/query-caching.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to query data from a Delta table as it existed 7 days ago using Time Travel. The `orders` table has been updated daily since then. Which query correctly reads the table as of 7 days ago?

options.text: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL 7 DAYS
SELECT * FROM orders TIMESTAMP AS OF DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE, 7)
SELECT * FROM orders VERSION AS OF 7
SELECT * FROM orders AT (TIMESTAMP => NOW() - INTERVAL 7 DAYS)

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because filtering by `order_date` column value is standard SQL filtering — it does not use Delta Time Travel to retrieve a historical table state.

**B** is correct because `TIMESTAMP AS OF <timestamp>` is the Delta Lake Time Travel syntax for reading a table as it existed at a specific point in time. `DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE, 7)` returns the date 7 days ago, giving the correct historical state.

**C** is incorrect because `VERSION AS OF 7` reads the table at Delta version number 7 (the 8th committed transaction), which is unlikely to correspond to the state from exactly 7 days ago — version numbers are sequential integers based on commits, not dates.

**D** is incorrect because `AT (TIMESTAMP =>` is not valid Spark SQL or Databricks SQL syntax. The correct Time Travel syntax is `TIMESTAMP AS OF <expr>`.

[Delta Time Travel — TIMESTAMP AS OF](https://docs.databricks.com/en/delta/history.html#delta-time-travel)

questions.stem:
A data analyst creates a view in Databricks SQL:

```sql
CREATE VIEW analytics.active_customers AS
SELECT customer_id, name, email
FROM raw.customers
WHERE status = 'active';
```

A colleague is granted SELECT on `analytics.active_customers`. Can the colleague access the `raw.customers` table directly?

options.text: No, granting SELECT on the view does not grant SELECT on the underlying raw.customers base table
Yes, because the view is built on raw.customers, so access to the view implies access to the base table
Yes, but only for the columns included in the view (customer_id, name, email)
No, the colleague cannot even use the view because they need raw.customers access first

questions.explanation:
**B** is incorrect because access to a view does NOT implicitly grant access to the underlying base tables. They are governed by separate permission grants.

**A** is correct because in Databricks SQL, granting `SELECT` on a view is independent of the base table permissions. The colleague can query the view and receive its results without having any direct permission on `raw.customers`.

**C** is incorrect because the colleague has no direct access to `raw.customers` at all — not even for the columns included in the view. Only view access is granted.

**D** is incorrect because the colleague CAN query the view without needing direct access to `raw.customers`. This is exactly the security benefit that makes views useful for access control.

[Views — access control and base table permissions](https://docs.databricks.com/en/sql/language-manual/sql-ref-syntax-ddl-create-view.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst runs `OPTIMIZE sales.orders ZORDER BY (customer_id, order_date);`. What does the ZORDER BY clause accomplish beyond the standard OPTIMIZE file compaction?

options.text: It sorts the data within each file by customer_id and order_date so that queries filtering by these columns can skip irrelevant files using data skipping
It creates a traditional B-tree index on customer_id and order_date for fast lookups
It partitions the table by customer_id and order_date, creating separate directories for each value combination
It materializes query results for all queries that filter on customer_id and order_date into a cache

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Z-Ordering is a multi-dimensional clustering technique that co-locates related values of the specified columns within the same data files. Delta Lake stores min/max statistics per file, and Z-Ordering ensures queries filtering on `customer_id` or `order_date` can skip entire files using data skipping, dramatically reducing data scanned.

**B** is incorrect because Delta Lake does not use traditional B-tree indexes. Z-Ordering uses file-level min/max statistics and data skipping — not an index data structure.

**C** is incorrect because Z-Ordering is not partitioning. It reorganizes data within the existing file layout without creating directory-based partition structures.

**D** is incorrect because Z-Ordering optimizes the physical data layout for efficient scanning — it has no effect on query result caching.

[OPTIMIZE with ZORDER BY — data skipping](https://docs.databricks.com/en/delta/optimize.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to create a materialized view in Databricks SQL to store pre-aggregated results for a frequently-run expensive query. Which statement correctly creates a materialized view?

options.text: CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW reporting.daily_sales AS SELECT order_date, SUM(revenue) FROM sales GROUP BY order_date;
Materialized views are not supported in Databricks SQL; pre-aggregated results must be stored as Delta tables
CREATE TABLE reporting.daily_sales AS SELECT order_date, SUM(revenue) FROM sales GROUP BY order_date;
CREATE VIEW MATERIALIZED reporting.daily_sales AS SELECT order_date, SUM(revenue) FROM sales GROUP BY order_date;

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because `CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW` is the valid Databricks SQL syntax for creating a materialized view — an object that stores pre-computed query results and can be automatically refreshed when the underlying data changes.

**D** is incorrect because `CREATE VIEW MATERIALIZED` has the keywords in the wrong order. The correct syntax is `CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW`.

**C** is incorrect because `CREATE TABLE ... AS SELECT` creates a static Delta table snapshot. Unlike a materialized view, it is not tracked as a view object and cannot be automatically refreshed to reflect source data changes.

**B** is incorrect because materialized views are supported in Databricks SQL as first-class objects, providing the convenience of a view with the performance of pre-materialized results.

[CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW](https://docs.databricks.com/en/sql/language-manual/sql-ref-syntax-ddl-create-materialized-view.html)

questions.stem:
A data analyst runs the following query against a Delta table:

```sql
SELECT * FROM sales.orders TIMESTAMP AS OF '2026-01-15 00:00:00';
```

The table has been VACUUM'd with the default 7-day retention. Today's date is 2026-06-20. What will happen?

options.text: The query succeeds and returns the table state as of January 15, 2026
The query fails because the historical data files for January 15 were removed by VACUUM
The query returns the current version of the table, ignoring the TIMESTAMP AS OF clause for past dates
The query succeeds because Delta always retains the full transaction log regardless of VACUUM

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because the data files required to reconstruct the table at January 15, 2026 have been deleted by VACUUM. Once files are vacuumed, historical versions that relied on those files cannot be read.

**B** is correct because VACUUM permanently removes data files older than the retention threshold (default 168 hours / 7 days). Since January 15 is more than 7 days ago and the table has been vacuumed since then, the historical data files are gone. Delta Time Travel requires the underlying Parquet data files to still exist.

**C** is incorrect because Databricks does not silently fall back to the current version when a Time Travel query cannot be satisfied. It raises an error indicating the historical data files are no longer available.

**D** is incorrect because the transaction log entries may still exist (log retention defaults to 30 days), but the actual Parquet data files needed to reconstruct the old version are deleted by VACUUM. Log entries without their data files cannot serve a Time Travel read.

[Delta Time Travel and VACUUM interaction](https://docs.databricks.com/en/delta/history.html#delta-time-travel)

questions.stem: A data analyst is working with an `orders` table that has a `discount_pct` column containing some null values (meaning no discount applies). the analyst needs a query that returns 0 when `discount_pct` is null and the actual value otherwise. additionally, the analyst wants to avoid division errors when computing `1 / discount_pct` — returning null when `discount_pct` is zero. which combination of functions handles both cases in a single expression?

options.text: COALESCE(discount_pct, 0) for the NULL case; NULLIF(discount_pct, 0) for the division case — these are separate expressions for separate needs
NULLIF(COALESCE(discount_pct, 0), 0) — this replaces NULL with 0 and then makes 0 NULL, which would always return NULL
COALESCE(discount_pct, 0) for display and 1 / NULLIF(discount_pct, 0) for the reciprocal calculation — each NULLIF/COALESCE serves its specific purpose
IFNULL(discount_pct, 0) handles both cases: NULL values become 0 and 0 values are also safe to divide by

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because while describing the functions correctly, this option does not address the combined need — it presents them as entirely independent when the question asks which combination handles both cases.

**B** is incorrect because nesting `NULLIF(COALESCE(discount_pct, 0), 0)` would convert NULL to 0 and then back to NULL — resulting in NULL for any NULL input, which defeats the purpose of showing 0 for the display case.

**C** is correct because the two requirements call for two different functions in different contexts: `COALESCE(discount_pct, 0)` for display (NULL becomes 0), and `1 / NULLIF(discount_pct, 0)` for the division calculation (0 becomes NULL to prevent division by zero).

**D** is incorrect because `IFNULL(discount_pct, 0)` replaces NULL with 0, but dividing by 0 would still raise a division-by-zero error — `IFNULL` provides no zero-division protection.

[COALESCE and NULLIF functions](https://docs.databricks.com/en/sql/language-manual/functions/coalesce.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst builds a dashboard with a databricks sql alert set to notify when daily revenue exceeds $1,000,000. the alert query runs on a scheduled refresh every day at 6 am. the alert is currently in the ok state (revenue was below $1m yesterday). today at 6 am the query runs and revenue is $1,200,000. the analyst manually changes the alert threshold to $1,500,000 at 7 am. at 6 am the next day, revenue is $1,300,000. will an alert notification be sent the next morning?

options.text: Yes, because $1,300,000 exceeds the original threshold of $1,000,000
Yes, because the alert was in TRIGGERED state and $1,300,000 is still above $1,000,000
No, because $1,300,000 does not exceed the updated threshold of $1,500,000, and the condition evaluates to OK
No, because once an alert is manually modified, it is paused until re-enabled

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because the original threshold of $1,000,000 was replaced by the updated $1,500,000 threshold. The alert evaluates against the current threshold, not historical ones.

**B** is incorrect because after the threshold was changed to $1,500,000, the alert state resets to evaluate against the new threshold. $1,300,000 is below $1,500,000, so the condition evaluates to OK — no notification.

**C** is correct because after updating the threshold to $1,500,000, the next morning's revenue of $1,300,000 does not meet the new threshold condition ($1,300,000 < $1,500,000). The alert evaluates to OK state and no notification is sent.

**D** is incorrect because modifying an alert threshold does not pause or disable the alert. The alert continues to run on schedule and evaluates against the updated threshold.

[Alert thresholds and state transitions](https://docs.databricks.com/en/sql/user/alerts/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to add interactivity to a dashboard so that viewers can narrow down a table of sales records by typing in a customer name. Which Databricks SQL parameter type is most appropriate for a free-text customer name search?

options.text: Date parameter
Number parameter
Text parameter
Query-based dropdown parameter

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because a Date parameter creates a date-picker widget and only accepts date values, making it unsuitable for free-text customer name input.

**B** is incorrect because a Number parameter accepts only numeric input — inappropriate for a text-based name search.

**C** is correct because a Text parameter creates a free-text input field where users can type any string value. The typed value can then be used in a `LIKE` or `ILIKE` clause in the query (e.g., `WHERE customer_name ILIKE '%{{customer_name}}%'`). This is the appropriate type for open-ended name searches.

**D** is incorrect because a query-based dropdown shows a discrete list of selectable values from a lookup query. For an open-ended customer name search with potentially thousands of names, a free-text input is more usable and practical than a dropdown.

[Parameterized queries — Text parameter](https://docs.databricks.com/en/sql/user/queries/queries.html#query-parameters)

questions.stem:
A data analyst writes the following query to find each customer's previous order date:

```sql
select
  customer_id,
  order_date,
  lag(order_date, 1) over (partition by customer_id order by order_date) as prev_order_date
from orders;
```

what value will `prev_order_date` contain for a customer's very first order?

options.text: The earliest order date across all customers
NULL, because there is no previous row for the first order
The same value as order_date for that row
0, because the default offset for LAG is zero

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because LAG is scoped within the `PARTITION BY customer_id` partition. It does not look across other customers' data to find an earliest date.

**B** is correct because `LAG(order_date, 1)` looks back one row within the customer's partition. For the first row in each partition, there is no preceding row, so `LAG` returns NULL by default.

**C** is incorrect because LAG does not default to the current row's value when no preceding row exists. The default return for a missing lag row is NULL unless a custom default is specified as the third argument.

**D** is incorrect because 0 would only be returned if the analyst explicitly specified `LAG(order_date, 1, 0)` with a default value of 0. Without a default argument, NULL is returned.

[Window function LAG](https://docs.databricks.com/en/sql/language-manual/functions/lag.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to look up the category name for each product. products without a category should still appear in the results with a null category name. the `products` table has `product_id` and `category_id`. the `categories` table has `category_id` and `category_name`. some products have a `category_id` that does not exist in the `categories` table. which join returns all products, including those with unmatched or null category ids?

options.text: INNER JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.category_id
LEFT JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.category_id
RIGHT JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.category_id
FULL OUTER JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.category_id

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because INNER JOIN returns only products that have a matching `category_id` in the `categories` table. Products with NULL or unmatched `category_id` values are excluded.

**B** is correct because LEFT JOIN preserves all rows from the left table (`products`) regardless of whether a match exists in `categories`. Products with NULL or unmatched `category_id` appear in the results with NULL values for `category_name`.

**C** is incorrect because RIGHT JOIN preserves all rows from the right table (`categories`) — including categories with no products — but may exclude products whose `category_id` is unmatched.

**D** is incorrect because FULL OUTER JOIN includes all products AND all categories. This adds extra rows for categories without any products, which exceeds the requirement of returning all products.

[LEFT JOIN — preserving all left-table rows](https://docs.databricks.com/en/sql/language-manual/sql-ref-syntax-qry-select-join.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst discovers that a batch job mistakenly overwrote a delta table 3 hours ago (the table was at version 42 before the overwrite, which created version 43). the analyst wants to restore the table to version 42. which sql command performs this recovery?

options.text: SELECT * FROM sales.orders VERSION AS OF 42
RESTORE TABLE sales.orders TO VERSION AS OF 42
INSERT OVERWRITE sales.orders SELECT * FROM sales.orders VERSION AS OF 42
ROLLBACK sales.orders TO VERSION 42

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because `SELECT ... VERSION AS OF 42` reads historical data from version 42 as a read-only query — it does not modify the current state of the table.

**B** is correct because `RESTORE TABLE ... TO VERSION AS OF 42` is the Delta Lake command that reverts a table to a specific historical version. After execution, the table's current state reflects version 42, and a new version entry (e.g., version 44) is recorded in the transaction log documenting the restore operation.

**C** is incorrect because while `INSERT OVERWRITE ... SELECT ... VERSION AS OF 42` would physically copy the historical data back, it is not the purpose-built restore command and does not record the operation as a restore in the Delta history.

**D** is incorrect because `ROLLBACK` is not a valid Delta Lake or Spark SQL command. The correct command for reverting to a historical version is `RESTORE TABLE`.

[Work with table history | Databricks on AWS](https://docs.databricks.com/en/delta/history.html)

questions.stem: A data engineering team has a large parquet dataset stored in cloud storage and wants to start using delta lake features (time travel, acid transactions, schema enforcement) on this data without migrating it to a new location. which sql command converts existing parquet files in-place to delta format?

options.text: ``ALTER TABLE parquet.`/path/to/data` SET FORMAT DELTA``
``CONVERT TO DELTA parquet.`/path/to/data` ``
``CREATE TABLE delta_table USING DELTA AS SELECT * FROM parquet.`/path/to/data```
``UPGRADE TABLE parquet.`/path/to/data` TO DELTA``

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because `ALTER TABLE ... SET FORMAT DELTA` is not a valid Databricks SQL command for format conversion.

**B** is correct because `CONVERT TO DELTA parquet.\`/path/to/data\`` is the Delta Lake command that converts existing Parquet files to Delta format in place. It adds a Delta transaction log (`_delta_log` directory) to the existing files without rewriting the data, enabling Delta features (Time Travel, ACID transactions, schema enforcement) immediately.

**C** is incorrect because `CREATE TABLE ... AS SELECT` creates a new Delta table by copying data from the Parquet source — the original Parquet files at their path are not converted in place.

**D** is incorrect because `UPGRADE TABLE ... TO DELTA` is not a valid Databricks SQL command. The correct command is `CONVERT TO DELTA`.

[CONVERT TO DELTA — in-place Parquet conversion](https://docs.databricks.com/en/delta/clone-parquet.html)
questions.reference: [Incrementally clone Parquet and Apache Iceberg tables to Delta Lake](https://docs.databricks.com/en/delta/clone-parquet.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst publishes a Databricks SQL dashboard and wants to ensure it always shows fresh data. The underlying queries take about 2 minutes to run. Select TWO TRUE statements about dashboard refresh behavior. (Select TWO)

options.text: A scheduled refresh will automatically re-run the underlying queries at the configured interval and update the dashboard cache
When a viewer opens the dashboard, it always triggers a fresh query execution regardless of the cache state
If a dashboard query ran 20 minutes ago and the result cache is still valid, viewers opening the dashboard will see the cached result without re-running the query
Dashboard scheduled refresh and the 24-hour query result cache operate independently — the scheduled refresh updates results even if the cache has not expired

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a scheduled dashboard refresh automatically re-runs the underlying queries at the configured interval and updates the cached results so viewers see current data without manually triggering a refresh.

**B** is incorrect because opening a dashboard does NOT always trigger fresh query execution. If valid cached results exist, viewers see those immediately — the purpose of caching is to avoid re-running queries on every page open.

**C** is incorrect as the primary answer because while this statement is factually true as a standalone fact, the question asks for the two TRUE statements that distinguish key dashboard refresh concepts — A and D are the better answers covering scheduled refresh behavior.

**D** is correct because the scheduled refresh and the 24-hour query result cache are independent mechanisms. A scheduled refresh explicitly triggers query re-execution and updates results regardless of whether the 24-hour cache TTL has expired or not.

[Dashboard refresh and query result cache](https://docs.databricks.com/en/dashboards/index.html)
